title: Least Squares Problems
objectives: Formulate the linear least squares problem geometrically and algebraically
Derive and solve the normal equations
Apply QR factorization for numerically stable least squares
Use the SVD and pseudoinverse for rank-deficient problems
Understand conditioning and error analysis for least squares

content.text: Many applications produce overdetermined linear systems—more equations than unknowns—which typically have no exact solution. Least squares finds the best approximate solution.

content.title: Linear Least Squares Problem
content.content:
Given $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ with $m > n$ and $b \in \mathbb{R}^m$, the **linear least squares problem** is:
\[
\min_{x \in \mathbb{R}^n} \|Ax - b\|_2^2
\]
The minimizer $x^*$ is called the **least squares solution**.

content.title: Data Fitting as Least Squares
Least Squares Geometry
content.content:
The least squares solution $x^*$ satisfies:

1. $Ax^*$ is the orthogonal projection of $b$ onto $\text{col}(A)$

2. The residual $r = b - Ax^*$ is orthogonal to $\text{col}(A)$

3. $\|b - Ax^*\|_2 = \text{dist}(b, \text{col}(A))$
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
The function $f(x) = \|Ax - b\|_2^2$ is minimized when $Ax^*$ is as close to $b$ as possible within $\text{col}(A)$. This occurs when $b - Ax^*$ is orthogonal to $\text{col}(A)$, i.e., orthogonal to every column $a_j$ of $A$.

This means $a_j^T(b - Ax^*) = 0$ for all $j$, or equivalently $A^T(b - Ax^*) = 0$.

content.title: Normal Equations
content.content:
The least squares solution $x^*$ satisfies:
\[
A^T A x^* = A^T b
\]
These are the **normal equations**. If $A$ has full column rank, then $A^T A$ is invertible and:
\[
x^* = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T b
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
From Theorem [thm:ls-geometry], $A^T(b - Ax^*) = 0$, which gives $A^T A x^* = A^T b$.

If $\text{rank}(A) = n$, then $A^T A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ has $\text{null}(A^T A) = \text{null}(A) = \{0\}$, so $A^T A$ is invertible.

content.title: Normal Equations Method
content.content:
algorithmic[1]
 Compute $C = A^T A$ $\mathcal{O}(mn^2)$
 Compute $d = A^T b$ $\mathcal{O}(mn)$
 Factor $C = LL^T$ (Cholesky) $\mathcal{O}(n^3/3)$
 Solve $LL^T x = d$ $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$
 $x$
algorithmic

content.title: Normal Equations Instability
content.content: The condition number satisfies $\kappa(A^T A) = \kappa(A)^2$. If $\kappa(A) = 10^8$, then $\kappa(A^T A) = 10^{16}$, which exceeds double precision! The normal equations can lose all accuracy for ill-conditioned problems.

content.title: Ill-Conditioning in Normal Equations
content.problem:
Consider fitting a polynomial $p(t) = c_0 + c_1 t + \cdots + c_9 t^9$ to data at $t = 0, 0.1, 0.2, \ldots, 1.0$.

The Vandermonde matrix $A$ with $a_{ij} = t_i^{j-1}$ has $\kappa_2(A) \approx 10^{12}$.

Normal equations: $\kappa_2(A^T A) \approx 10^{24}$—completely unusable in standard precision.

QR-based methods work with $\kappa_2(A) \approx 10^{12}$, which is difficult but potentially manageable.

content.text: The QR factorization provides a more stable way to solve least squares problems.

content.title: Least Squares via QR
content.content:
Let $A = QR$ where $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ has orthonormal columns and $R \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is upper triangular. The least squares solution is:
\[
x^* = R^{-1} Q^T b
\]
equivalently, solve $Rx^* = Q^T b$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Since $Q$ has orthonormal columns:
\[
\|Ax - b\|_2^2 = \|QRx - b\|_2^2 = \|Rx - Q^T b\|_2^2 + \|b - QQ^T b\|_2^2
\]

The first term is minimized (to zero, if $R$ is invertible) by $Rx = Q^T b$. The second term is the squared distance from $b$ to $\text{col}(A) = \text{col}(Q)$, independent of $x$.

content.title: QR Method for Least Squares
content.content:
algorithmic[1]
 Factor $A = QR$ (Householder) $\mathcal{O}(2mn^2 - 2n^3/3)$
 Compute $c = Q^T b$ $\mathcal{O}(2mn)$
 Solve $Rx = c$ (back substitution) $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$
 $x$, residual $\|b\|_2^2 - \|c_{1:n}\|_2^2$
algorithmic

content.title: Least Squares via QR
content.solution:
**QR Factorization:**
$A = QR$ where (computing via Gram-Schmidt or Householder):
\[
Q = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \cdot \text{normalization}, \quad
R = \begin{pmatrix} \sqrt{3} & 2\sqrt{3} \\ 0 & \sqrt{2} \end{pmatrix}
\]

Actually: $q_1 = (1,1,1)^T/\sqrt{3}$, and $a_2 - (q_1^T a_2)q_1 = (1,2,3)^T - 2(1,1,1)^T = (-1,0,1)^T$, giving $q_2 = (-1,0,1)^T/\sqrt{2}$.

So $R = \begin{pmatrix} \sqrt{3} & 6/\sqrt{3} \\ 0 & \sqrt{2} \end{pmatrix}$.

**Solve:**
$c = Q^T b = \begin{pmatrix} (1+2+2)/\sqrt{3} \\ (-1+0+2)/\sqrt{2} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 5/\sqrt{3} \\ 1/\sqrt{2} \end{pmatrix}$

Back substitution: $\sqrt{2} x_2 = 1/\sqrt{2} \Rightarrow x_2 = 1/2$

$\sqrt{3} x_1 + (6/\sqrt{3})(1/2) = 5/\sqrt{3} \Rightarrow x_1 = 5/3 - 1 = 2/3$

Solution: $x^* = (2/3, 1/2)^T$.

content.text: For rank-deficient problems, the SVD provides the most robust approach.

content.title: Least Squares via SVD
content.content:
Let $A = U\Sigma V^T$ with $\text{rank}(A) = r$. The minimum-norm least squares solution is:
\[
x^* = A^+ b = V\Sigma^+ U^T b = \sum_{i=1}^r \frac{u_i^T b}{\sigma_i} v_i
\]
The residual is $\|b - Ax^*\|_2 = \sqrt{\sum_{i=r+1}^m (u_i^T b)^2}$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: SVD Method for Least Squares
content.content:
algorithmic[1]
 Compute SVD: $A = U\Sigma V^T$ $\mathcal{O}(mn^2 + n^3)$
 $c \gets U^T b$
$i = 1, \ldots, r$
 $y_i \gets c_i / \sigma_i$

 $y_{r+1:n} \gets 0$ Minimum norm choice
 $x \gets Vy$
 $x$
algorithmic

content.text: When the problem is ill-conditioned, small singular values amplify noise. **Regularization** addresses this by damping small singular value contributions.

content.title: Truncated SVD
content.content:
The **truncated SVD (TSVD)** solution uses only the $k$ largest singular values:
\[
x_k = \sum_{i=1}^k \frac{u_i^T b}{\sigma_i} v_i = V_k \Sigma_k^{-1} U_k^T b
\]
Terms with small $\sigma_i$ (which amplify noise) are discarded.

content.title: Tikhonov Regularization
content.content:
**Tikhonov regularization** (also called ridge regression) solves:
\[
\min_x \|Ax - b\|_2^2 + \lambda^2 \|x\|_2^2
\]
The solution is:
\[
x_\lambda = (A^T A + \lambda^2 I)^{-1} A^T b = \sum_{i=1}^r \frac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_i^2 + \lambda^2} (u_i^T b) v_i
\]

content.title: Tikhonov as Filtered SVD
content.content: Tikhonov regularization applies a **filter function** $\phi_i = \sigma_i^2 / (\sigma_i^2 + \lambda^2)$ to each SVD component. As $\lambda \to 0$, $x_\lambda \to x^*$. As $\lambda \to \infty$, $x_\lambda \to 0$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Effect of Regularization
content.problem:
For a problem with singular values $\sigma = (10, 1, 0.1, 0.01)$ and noisy data:

| $\lambda$ | $\phi_1$ | $\phi_2$ | $\phi_3$ | $\phi_4$ | Effect |
| 0 | 1.00 | 1.00 | 1.00 | 1.00 | Unregularized (noisy) |
| 0.1 | 1.00 | 0.99 | 0.50 | 0.01 | Damps small $\sigma$ |
| 1 | 0.99 | 0.50 | 0.01 | 0.00 | Strong regularization |

Regularization trades bias (systematic error) for reduced variance (noise sensitivity).

content.title: Least Squares Sensitivity
content.content:
For the least squares problem with solution $x^*$ and residual $r = b - Ax^*$:

1. Perturbations in $b$: $\frac{\|\delta x\|}{\|x^*\|} \lesssim \frac{\kappa(A)}{\cos\theta} \frac{\|\delta b\|}{\|b\|}$

2. Perturbations in $A$: $\frac{\|\delta x\|}{\|x^*\|} \lesssim \left(\kappa(A)^2 \tan\theta + \kappa(A)\right) \frac{\|\delta A\|}{\|A\|}$
where $\theta$ is the angle between $b$ and $\text{col}(A)$, with $\tan\theta = \|r\|_2 / \|Ax^*\|_2$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Gauss and Least Squares
content.content: Carl Friedrich Gauss developed the method of least squares around 1795 to determine the orbit of Ceres from limited astronomical observations. Legendre independently published the method in 1805. Gauss later showed that least squares gives the optimal linear unbiased estimator when errors are normally distributed—the foundation of regression analysis.
